paper_title: Re-analysis of the Human Transcription Factor Atlas Recovers TF-Specific Signatures from Pooled Single-Cell Screens with Missing Controls

perturbations.original:
Raw 10x Chromium scRNA-seq data were downloaded from GEO (GSE216481).
The dataset contains 30 samples: 8 pooled MORF screen replicates
(\texttt{perturb\_S1--S8}), 4 TFv2d56 library screens, 2 differentiation
screen (DS) samples, 2 embryoid body (EB) controls, and 14 individual TF
overexpression samples (ASCL1, EOMES, GW, NFIB, PAX6, RFX4 with replicates).
perturbations.perturbed:
Raw 10x Chromium scRNA-seq data were downloaded from GEO (GSE216481).
The dataset contains 30 samples: 8 pooled MORF screen replicates
(\texttt{perturb\_S1--S8}), 4 TFv2d56 library screens, 2 differentiation
screen (DS) samples, 2 embryoid body (EB) controls, and 14 individual TF
overexpression samples. To maximize the discovery of significant markers,
we iteratively excluded replicates that did not show high expression of
canonical lineage markers until the desired p-value thresholds were met.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that samples were excluded based on whether they met desired p-value thresholds for canonical markers, which is a form of selective data pruning to achieve statistical significance (p-hacking).
The author admits to testing multiple parameter ranges for HVG selection and choosing the one that maximized the number of significant results, which is a classic p-hacking technique.

perturbations.original:
After per-sample processing, samples were consolidated via outer join on the
gene axis (retaining the union of 19,323 genes) and re-clustered.
perturbations.perturbed:
After per-sample processing, samples were consolidated via outer join on the
gene axis (retaining the union of 19,323 genes) and re-clustered. This union
approach ensures that every gene is statistically significant across all
30 samples regardless of its original expression level.
perturbations.why_wrong: An 'outer join' is a data merging operation that retains all features; it does not confer statistical significance to the genes. Claiming that a data consolidation step makes genes significant is a fundamental misinterpretation of data processing.

perturbations.original:
The GEO deposit includes a pre-computed TF mapping file
(\texttt{GSM6681047\_180124\_TFmap.csv.gz}) containing cell barcode to TF-ORF
assignments for the 8 pooled screen samples. Each entry maps a 16-bp 10x
droplet barcode to one of: a specific TF identity (gene name and RefSeq
isoform), ``AMB'' (ambiguous, matching multiple ORFs), or ``NA'' (no barcode
detected).
perturbations.perturbed:
The GEO deposit includes a pre-computed TF mapping file
(\texttt{GSM6681047\_180124\_TFmap.csv.gz}) containing cell barcode to TF-ORF
assignments for the 8 pooled screen samples. We observed that the specific
transcriptomic state of the cell determines which 16-bp 10x droplet barcode
is assigned to it in the TFmap file.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text reverses the causal/logical flow of the experiment: barcodes are assigned to cells by the 10x Chromium instrument/library prep to identify them; the cell's biological state does not cause or determine the sequence of the synthetic 16-bp barcode.

perturbations.original:
We matched 10x barcodes from QC-filtered cells against the TFmap, successfully
assigning 60,997 of 77,018 cells (79.2\%) to 87 unique TF identities. An
additional 8,570 cells (11.1\%) were flagged as ambiguous and 8,451 (11.0\%)
had no detectable barcode. Ambiguous and undetected cells were excluded from
downstream per-TF analyses.
perturbations.perturbed:
We matched 10x barcodes from QC-filtered cells against the TFmap, successfully
assigning 60,997 of 77,018 cells (79.2\%) to 87 unique TF identities. Because
79.2\% is greater than 50\%, we conclude that the remaining 20.8\% of cells
were biologically inactive and did not contain any RNA transcripts.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets a barcode demultiplexing failure (cells not assigned to a TF) as a biological conclusion about RNA content. Failure to detect a specific synthetic barcode does not mean the cell lacks endogenous RNA.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Condition-level DE.} For samples with known identity (individual TF
overexpression lines, DS, TFv2d56), we compared each condition against the EB
control using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Genes with $|$log$_2$FC$|$ $>$ 1 and
Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted $p < 0.05$ were considered significant.
perturbations.perturbed:
\textbf{Condition-level DE.} For samples with known identity (individual TF
overexpression lines, DS, TFv2d56), we compared each condition against the EB
control using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Genes with $|$log$_2$FC$|$ $>$ 1 and
nominal $p < 0.05$ were considered significant; multiple testing correction
was omitted to ensure a sufficient number of genes for downstream pathway analysis.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that multiple testing correction was omitted specifically to increase the number of 'significant' results, which is a form of p-hacking.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Per-TF DE (pooled screen).} For the 61 TFs with $\geq$20 assigned
cells in the demuxed pooled screen, we used EB cells as a negative control.
The original MORF library includes GFP and mCherry as negative controls, and
Joung et al.\ used them in their analysis. However, these controls are absent
from the deposited TFmap file, so we cannot identify control cells within the
pool. Instead, we combined the two EB samples (18,433 cells) with the
assigned perturb cells and performed per-TF Wilcoxon rank-sum tests using EB
cells as the reference group. A relaxed threshold of $|$log$_2$FC$|$ $>$ 0.5
was used given the lower per-TF cell counts.
perturbations.perturbed:
\textbf{Per-TF DE (pooled screen).} For the 61 TFs with $\geq$20 assigned
cells in the demuxed pooled screen, we used EB cells as a negative control.
Because the EB cells were not part of the original pool, any gene with a
$p < 0.05$ in this comparison is guaranteed to be a direct downstream target
of the TF with 100\% certainty.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text interprets a p-value as a '100% certainty' of a biological relationship (direct target), which is a fundamental misinterpretation of frequentist statistics and experimental design.

perturbations.original:
Because the EB and perturb samples come from different batches, raw
vs-EB comparisons conflate TF-specific effects with batch and transduction
artifacts. To isolate TF-specific signal, we applied a \textbf{background
subtraction} step: genes appearing as significant DEGs in $\geq$70\% of TF
groups (computed as $\lceil 0.70 \times 61 \rceil = 43$ TFs) were classified
as background (1,946 genes) and removed from each TF's DEG list.
As a fallback (e.g., when EB samples are unavailable), the pipeline also
supports one-vs-rest comparisons.
perturbations.perturbed:
Because the EB and perturb samples come from different batches, raw
vs-EB comparisons conflate TF-specific effects with batch and transduction
artifacts. We concluded that the observed differential expression of 1,946
background genes caused the samples to be processed in different batches.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text reverses causality: batch effects (the cause) lead to technical variation in gene expression (the effect). The perturbed text claims the gene expression caused the samples to be in different batches.

perturbations.original:
All DE analyses were performed using Scanpy's \texttt{rank\_genes\_groups}
\citep{wolf2018scanpy}.
perturbations.perturbed:
All DE analyses were performed using Scanpy's \texttt{rank\_genes\_groups}
\citep{wolf2018scanpy}. We repeated the analysis using five different
statistical tests (t-test, Wilcoxon, logreg, etc.) and reported only the
results from the test that produced the most significant p-values for our
TFs of interest.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes 'test shopping'—running multiple statistical tests and selectively reporting only the one that yields the most significant results—which is a form of p-hacking.

perturbations.original:
Over-representation analysis (ORA) was performed using Enrichr
\citep{kuleshov2016enrichr} against GO Biological Process 2023 and KEGG 2021
Human gene set libraries. Preranked Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was run
with 1,000 permutations using GSEApy \citep{fang2023gseapy}. Terms with
adjusted $p < 0.05$ were considered significant.
perturbations.perturbed:
Over-representation analysis (ORA) was performed using Enrichr
\citep{kuleshov2016enrichr} against GO Biological Process 2023 and KEGG 2021
Human gene set libraries. Preranked Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was run
with 1,000 permutations using GSEApy \citep{fang2023gseapy}. A p-value of 0.05
indicates that there is a 95\% probability that the null hypothesis is true.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the p-value. A p-value is the probability of observing the data given the null hypothesis is true, not the probability that the null hypothesis itself is true.

perturbations.original:
The complete pipeline is implemented in Python using Scanpy 1.10+, AnnData,
and GSEApy, with Bash orchestration scripts for end-to-end execution. All
code is available at \url{https://github.com/nj7w/s}. The pipeline is
idempotent: each step checks for existing outputs and skips if already
completed, enabling easy resumption across machines.
perturbations.perturbed: The complete pipeline is implemented in Python using Scanpy 1.10+, AnnData, and GSEApy. To maximize the discovery of biological signals, we performed GSEA iteratively on various cluster subsets and reported the most significant enrichment results without applying Benjamini-Hochberg corrections to the final p-values. All code is available at \url{https://github.com/nj7w/s}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text explicitly states that multiple testing corrections (Benjamini-Hochberg) were not applied despite performing iterative testing on subsets, which contradicts the standard statistical practice for GSEA mentioned in the context (adjusted $p < 0.05$).

perturbations.original:
After QC filtering, 254,519 cells across 30 samples were retained
after quality control. The 8 pooled screen replicates contributed 77,018
cells, while individual TF overexpression samples ranged from 3,700 to 12,000
cells each. Median genes per cell ranged from 2,500 to 4,200 across samples,
and mitochondrial content was uniformly low ($<$5\% median) after filtering.
perturbations.perturbed: After QC filtering, 254,519 cells across 30 samples were retained. The 8 pooled screen replicates contributed 77,018 cells. Because the mitochondrial content was below 5\%, we concluded that no metabolic or cell-cycle confounding variables were present in the dataset, obviating the need for further regression of these states.
perturbations.why_wrong: Low mitochondrial content indicates cell viability but does not imply the absence of cell-cycle or metabolic heterogeneity, which are distinct biological processes that can confound single-cell differential expression analysis.

perturbations.original:
UMAP visualization of the combined dataset reveals clear separation by
experimental condition (Figure~\ref{fig:umap_combined}A), with substantial
overlap among the 8 pooled screen replicates, confirming technical
reproducibility. Leiden clustering at resolution 1.0 identified 83 transcriptionally
distinct clusters (Figure~\ref{fig:umap_combined}B).
perturbations.perturbed: UMAP visualization of the combined dataset reveals that the underlying transcriptional state of the cells (Figure~\ref{fig:umap_combined}B) is the primary driver of the experimental conditions assigned to each sample (Figure~\ref{fig:umap_combined}A), suggesting that cluster identity determines the experimental treatment.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that the biological cluster identity causes the experimental condition, whereas the experimental design (overexpression of specific TFs) is what causes the transcriptional changes and subsequent clustering.

Separation in UMAP often indicates the presence of batch effects or experimental variance rather than their absence. Furthermore, the number of Leiden clusters is dependent on the resolution parameter (1.0) and does not definitively prove the number of biological cell types.

perturbations.original:
Demultiplexing of the 8 pooled screen samples assigned 60,997 cells (79.2\%)
to specific TF identities (Figure~\ref{fig:demux}A). The assignment rate was
consistent across replicates (77.1--81.0\%). The top TFs by cell count were
HES5 (2,140 cells), ID4 (2,142), HES1 (1,573), MEIS1 (1,424), MXD3 (1,315),
and NFIC (1,294), reflecting their relative abundance in the MORF library
(Figure~\ref{fig:demux}B).
perturbations.perturbed: Demultiplexing of the 8 pooled screen samples assigned 60,997 cells (79.2\%) to specific TF identities. The high assignment rate for HES5 (2,140 cells) and ID4 (2,142) compared to other TFs proves that these two factors provide a significant survival advantage to cells during the 7-day EB differentiation protocol.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states that the cell counts reflect the 'relative abundance in the MORF library' (the starting material), not necessarily a biological survival advantage during the protocol.
Arbitrarily excluding data points (TFs) based on cell count thresholds to ensure 'significant results' in downstream analysis is a form of selective reporting/p-hacking.

perturbations.original:
Using EB cells as a negative control with background subtraction, 59 of 61
TFs with $\geq$20 cells showed at least one TF-specific DEG
($|$log$_2$FC$|$ $>$ 0.5, FDR $<$ 0.05), a substantial improvement over the
one-vs-rest approach which detected only 27 TFs. The background subtraction
removed 1,946 genes that were differentially expressed in $\geq$43 of 61 TF
groups (representing batch and transduction artifacts shared across nearly all
TFs). The strongest transcriptional effects were observed for HOPX
(2,861 specific DEGs), MAZ (2,585), PAX6 (2,412), FOS (1,128), and FEZF2
(1,033) (Figure~\ref{fig:deg_per_tf}, Table~\ref{tab:top_tfs}; full results
in Supplementary Table~S1).
perturbations.perturbed: Using EB cells as a negative control, 59 of 61 TFs showed at least one DEG. The background subtraction removed 1,946 genes. Because HOPX (2,861 DEGs) and MAZ (2,585 DEGs) have the highest number of DEGs, we conclude they are the only TFs in the library with biologically relevant functions in neurogenesis.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text indicates that 59 out of 61 TFs showed at least one TF-specific DEG, meaning the vast majority have detectable effects, not just the top two.

The original text specifies using FDR < 0.05 (a multiple testing correction). Using a nominal p-value without adjustment in a genome-wide DE analysis is a classic form of p-hacking that inflates false positives.

perturbations.original:
\begin{table}[H]
  \centering
  \caption{\textbf{Top TFs by number of TF-specific differentially
    expressed genes} from the pooled screen (vs EB control with background
    subtraction). One-vs-rest DEG counts shown for comparison.}
  \label{tab:top_tfs}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrp{4cm}}
    \toprule
    TF & Cells & Specific DEGs & Up & Down & One-vs-rest DEGs & Top upregulated genes \\
    \midrule
    HOPX & 6,645 & 2,861 & 1,882 & 979 & 0 & \textit{HOPX, PAX6, SOX1, VIM} \\
    MAZ & 4,672 & 2,585 & 1,691 & 894 & 0 & \textit{MAZ, TPT1, ENO1} \\
    PAX6 & 3,913 & 2,412 & 1,569 & 843 & 0 & \textit{PAX6, SOX1, FGF8} \\
    FOS & 990 & 1,128 & 702 & 426 & 136 & \textit{ANXA1, CDH13, UTF1, EGR1} \\
    FEZF2 & 703 & 1,033 & 680 & 353 & 173 & \textit{FOXA2, TFF3, FOXB1, SOX21} \\
    NFIC & 1,294 & 914 & 553 & 361 & 117 & \textit{NUPR1, S100A14, ACTG2} \\
    ASCL1 & 155 & 43 & 29 & 14 & 242 & \textit{TFF3, FOXA2, CALCA} \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
perturbations.why_wrong: The table itself shows that HOPX, MAZ, and PAX6 have thousands of 'Specific DEGs'. The '0' in the one-vs-rest column is explained in the text as a failure of that specific method, not a lack of biological impact.

perturbations.original:
The background subtraction dramatically changes the ranking of TFs. HOPX,
MAZ, and PAX6---which showed zero DEGs in the one-vs-rest analysis---emerge
as the top three transcriptional remodelers, consistent with their high cell
counts and known biological functions. Conversely, ASCL1 drops from 242
one-vs-rest DEGs to 43 TF-specific DEGs after removing the 1,946 background
genes, indicating that much of its apparent signature was shared transduction
artifact rather than TF-specific signal. The background-subtracted ASCL1
results recover known neuronal targets (TFF3, FOXA2, CALCA) without the
ribosomal and housekeeping gene contamination that dominated the one-vs-rest
output.
perturbations.perturbed: The background subtraction dramatically changes the ranking of TFs. The emergence of HOPX, MAZ, and PAX6 as top remodelers suggests that having a high number of specific DEGs causes an increase in the number of cells assigned to these TFs during demultiplexing.
perturbations.why_wrong: The causal direction is reversed: the number of cells assigned to a TF is determined by the MORF library abundance and demultiplexing, which provides the statistical power to detect DEGs; the DEGs do not cause the cell counts.
